title: Getting started with first-class quality on Trade-in
bodyHtml:
This article covers the “Processing your first Trade-in orders” module of the Trade-in Launch Phase. To complete this section and get 5 Trade-in orders added to your daily limit, you need to your quality metrics below the thresholds 2 weeks after you generated your first 15 Trade-in orders:

 

  • Counteroffer Rate below 33%
  • Late Processing Rate below 15%
  • Late Response Rate below 15%

Learn more about Trade-in Launch Phase

 

Customer service management is as crucial for Trade-in as it is for sales. As a Back Market partner, you have to deliver a seamless experience to the customer when processing the order and answering requests.

Keep in mind that Trade-in isn’t included in Customer Care by Back Market, which means you’re the one chatting with and supporting Back Market customers.

Here are the golden rules you need to respect to succeed in Trade-in.

 

Keep a low number of counteroffers

Counteroffers are critical to customer satisfaction and require extra effort from you, which is why you need to avoid them whenever possible. It’s recommended that your counteroffer rate remains around 20% of received orders. To achieve this, adjust your quality checks to be less strict and overlook some small defects that have low-cost impacts for you based on your area of expertise (for example, microphone issues, minor screen scratches when none are expected, etc.).

Quality metric this impacts: Counteroffer Rate

How this rate is calculated: We count all the CO you made for the orders received in the last 30 days. We add an estimation of still-undeclared CO for your orders pending processing or pending a customer action by applying your raw CO rate* to them. We divide by the number of orders you received in the last 30 days.
The Counteroffer rate is calculated every day.

*raw CO rate is the old definition of the CO rate, computed as: Number of CO you made for the orders received in the last 30 days, divided by the number of orders you received in the last 30 days.

Where to track this rate: You can access it from your sales Back Office > “Insights” > “Trade-in”.

How to keep this rate low: Adjust your decision-making on counteroffers and listing pricing knowing that phones traded-in are never brand new and Back Market customers tend to overestimate their value. Also, regularly check your Counteroffer Rate. Get more advice here

What happens if this rate is too high:

 

  • The higher your counteroffer rate is, the higher the prices for sourcing will be for you. Indeed, since the launch of the Trade-in BackBox as of June 2025, you are rewarded for having a lower counteroffer rate than your competitors by being able to access the BackBox for a lower price. Learn more about the Trade-in BackBox.
  • Also, Learn about the monitoring period and limits here

Process all orders within 48 hours of receiving them

Processing an order means taking action on the order (confirming payment, making a counteroffer, asking for more information, or reporting the product unprocessable). This timeline excludes weekends and official holidays.

Quality metric this impacts: Late Processing Rate

How this rate is calculated: Number of Trade-in orders that were processed too late (after 48 hours) in the last 30 days divided by the number of Trade-in orders in the last 30 days. This rate is updated every day.

Where to track this rate: You can access it from your sales Back Office > “Insights” > “Trade-in”.

How to keep this rate low: Check your Back Office every day by going to "Trade-in" > "Orders" > "New orders". You'll also see a timer there showing how much time you have left to process each order. Find more advice here

What happens if this rate is too high: If your Late Processing Rate goes over 15%, you’ll enter a monitoring period. Learn about the monitoring period and limits here

 

Answer all customer requests within 24 hours

A valid response includes any action on the order (like the ones for processing the order) or an answer in the order chat. This timeline excludes weekends and official holidays.

Quality metric this impacts: Late Response Rate

How this rate is calculated: Number of Trade-in orders with a late seller response divided by all Trade-in orders with a customer message. This rate is updated every day based on all orders received in the last 30 days.

Where to track this rate: You can access it from your sales Back Office > “Insights” > “Trade-in”.

How to keep this rate low: Start with staying on top of your Back Office and check your “Orders to reply to” inbox several times a day. Find more advice here

What happens if this rate is too high: If your Late Response Rate goes over 15%, you’ll enter a monitoring period. Learn about the monitoring period and limits here

 

More best practices and keys to success

You’ll be off to a good start with these rules, but we strongly recommend you follow these practices too to ensure good quality.

Read the Back Market Quality Charter from beginning to end and respect it.
Start by reading the Trade-in part of the Quality Charter (starting from page 36)
And check out the summary in Part 2: Trade-in

If we notice a serious breach with our Quality Charter, supported by at least 3 cases, you’ll be immediately placed in a monitoring period. If you don’t respond, your daily Trade-in limit will decrease, and you might be removed from Trade-in.

Handle your counteroffers properly and keep your Counteroffer Acceptance Rate high.
If a Back Market customer rejects your counteroffer, it’s bad for both you and the customer. Also, less than 1% of Back Market customers are satisfied after rejecting a counteroffer. That’s why you need to avoid this situation.

To help you monitor this, your Counteroffer Acceptance Rate measures the percentage of orders with counteroffers that were accepted in the last 30 days.

To keep this rate high, here are some tips:

 

  • Explain the issue clearly and thoroughly to avoid questions and/or exchanges with the Back Market customer.
  • Justify each appearance issue identified with a photo, video, or test report for technical defects as the Back Market customer will expect this from you to accept your counteroffer.
  • Specify the repair cost for each issue identified, so the Back Market customer will understand the new amount.

Get more advice here

Anticipate holidays and vacation.
If you plan to go on vacation or be away from your business, make sure to pause your Trade-in listings at least 2 weeks before your departure, to have almost no new orders received while you’re offline. Here’s how to do it from your Back Office or with a CSV file upload

Remember all orders received during this time and messages from Back Market customers must continue to be managed, so make sure you have resources to handle them during this period before you go.

Ship back products from declined offers in less than 48 hours.
If your counteroffer is declined, you have to do the following in the next 48 hours:

 

  • Generate a shipping label.
  • Send the product back to the Back Market customer within 2 business days. 
  • Provide the Back Market customer with the tracking number in the order chat.

Note that you can request a refund for the cost of shipping a product back in the Seller Support Center.

Learn about the return process for Trade-in order cancellations

Take advantage of our quality processes.
Monitoring and last-chance periods

When 1 of your quality metrics goes over the limit, you’ll be placed in a monitoring period, giving you 28 days to work on getting the metric below the allowed limit. If your rate doesn’t improve enough, your daily Trade-in limit will decrease in a second phase called the last-chance period, where you’re at risk of leaving Trade-in.

Learn about the monitoring period and limits here

Proactive interventions
To improve the Trade-in experience, we sometimes help resolve painful situations by intervening in order chats (or via email). This may include reminding you to act on Trade-in orders. These interventions only apply to the most painful situations identified by Back Market.

Learn about these interventions here
